summary.en: Set against beautiful, tree-covered hills, Arashiyama is a district defined by its breathtaking natural wonders and historic charm. It feels like a scenic escape from the urban grid.
Central Kyoto represents a collision of tradition and the modern world, anchored by the ultra-modern Kyoto Station. It is a bustling hub for shopping, nightlife, and modern convenience.
Gion is Kyoto's famous geisha district, offering a glimpse into traditional Japanese arts and culture. Its historic streets are perfect for atmospheric evening walks.
Nestled between the Kamo River and temple-studded mountains, Higashiyama is the historic, quintessential Kyoto you imagine. It's incredibly popular for evening strolls and cherry blossom viewing.

Deeply historic and spiritual, Northern Kyoto is graced with scores of centuries-old shrines and major World Heritage Sites. It offers opportunities to immerse yourself in traditional Japanese culture and Zen practices.
Covering a massive southern stretch of the city, this area mixes large, impressive temple grounds with a deep, centuries-old history of local brewing.

description.en: A charming cafe nestled near the iconic bamboo grove, offering a tranquil setting to enjoy refreshments amidst nature.
An iconic natural landmark featuring towering bamboo stalks that create a serene and otherworldly atmosphere, perfect for a peaceful stroll.
The streets around the station are lined with authentic, non-tacky local shops selling traditional crafts, fans, and sweets.
The area around Hankyu Arashiyama Station is filled with local food stalls at night during the blossom seasons, offering a variety of delicious snacks.
A convenient cafe located near the Sagano Romantic Train station, perfect for a quick drink or snack before or after your scenic ride.
One of Kyoto's most important Zen temples and a World Heritage site, known for its stunning landscape garden and historical significance.
An active film studio that is the traditional home of Japanese period dramas (jidaigeki), where you can walk through authentic outdoor samurai movie sets.
A renowned restaurant specializing in Yudofu, a traditional hot pot dish featuring delicate boiled tofu, a local specialty of Arashiyama.
The northern section of this park is home to a variety of different types of cherry blossoms, offering a beautiful natural escape.
This street is the vibrant center of Kyoto's night scene, packed with local watering holes catering to every vibe.
The ultra-modern glass-and-steel sprawl of Kyoto Station serves as a major transportation hub and a symbol of the city's blend of tradition and modernity.
A former residence of the Tokugawa shōguns, this castle is particularly striking at night in the spring when it hosts an illuminated cherry blossom walk.
Located on the 10th and 11th floors of the Isetan department store at Kyoto Station, it offers a veritable collection of distinct Japanese ramen styles.
This main street is a prominent area for shopping, with Teramachi Street branching off it.
A legendary live music venue, Taku Taku is mentioned as a staple of Kyoto's rock scene.
This covered shopping street off Shijo-dori is a hidden gem for manga and anime enthusiasts, featuring multi-story shops and collectibles.
A highly-rated restaurant known for its delicious Tonkatsu (fried pork cutlet), offering a classic Japanese comfort food experience near Kyoto Station.
A local craft beer pub offering a variety of unique and refreshing brews, popular among locals and visitors looking for a modern drinking experience.
An ultra-modern glass-and-steel architectural marvel, representing Kyoto's collision of tradition and the modern world, offering various shops and dining options.
A former residence of the Tokugawa shōguns, particularly striking at night in the spring when it hosts an illuminated cherry blossom walk.
A narrow, atmospheric alley running parallel to the Kamo River, packed with traditional restaurants and bars, offering a vibrant nightlife experience.
Located on the 10th and 11th floors of the Isetan department store attached to Kyoto Station, this spot offers a veritable collection of distinct Japanese ramen styles.
A legendary live music venue mentioned as a staple of Kyoto's rock scene, offering a vibrant cultural experience.
A covered shopping street off Shijo-dori that is a hidden gem for manga and anime enthusiasts, featuring a two-story Gamers branch and various collectible shops.
Known as the Silver Pavilion, this Zen temple is connected to Nanzen-ji by the Philosopher's Path.
Kyoto's famous geisha district, popular for evening strolls and experiencing traditional Japanese culture.
Located on Kiyomizu-zaka, this is the best place to find yatsuhashi—a traditional Kyoto sweet, with seasonal flavors like sakura and matcha.
A magnificent World Heritage temple famous for its massive wooden veranda that offers panoramic views overlooking the city.
The centerpiece of this park is a massive, famous cherry tree that is illuminated at night, making it a popular spot for blossom viewing.
During cherry blossom season, Maruyama Park is surrounded by festive food stalls offering a variety of delicious snacks.
A significant Zen Buddhist temple, it marks one end of the scenic Philosopher's Path.
This serene, cherry-tree-lined walking route connects Nanzen-ji to Ginkaku-ji, allowing visitors to discover various smaller shrines along the way.
Explore the numerous small, intimate bars tucked away in Gion's backstreets, offering a chance to experience local nightlife and traditional Japanese hospitality.
Experience an elaborate kaiseki dinner, a multi-course traditional Japanese meal, which is an essential part of a cultural stay in Kyoto.
Visit a traditional ochaya, a teahouse where geisha and maiko entertain guests with traditional music, dance, and conversation, often including light meals.
Discover a cozy sake bar in Gion to sample a variety of local and regional sakes, often paired with small traditional snacks.
The most famous street in Gion, lined with traditional wooden machiya houses, teahouses, and exclusive restaurants, where you might spot a geisha or maiko.
Kyoto's oldest Zen temple, featuring beautiful Zen gardens, a stunning twin dragon painting on the ceiling, and a serene atmosphere for contemplation.
A picturesque area along the Shirakawa Canal, known for its willow trees, stone bridges, and traditional architecture, offering a tranquil and scenic walk.
A prominent Shinto shrine located at the eastern end of Shijo-dori, famous for its beautiful main hall and vibrant festivals, especially Gion Matsuri.
A popular minimalist coffee shop known for its high-quality coffee and picturesque views, offering a modern cafe experience in a historic district.
Located on Kiyomizu-zaka, this specific local shop is the best place to find yatsuhashi—a traditional Kyoto sweet. You can sample seasonal flavors like sakura, matcha, and black poppyseed.
A magnificent World Heritage temple famous for its massive wooden veranda that overlooks the city, offering stunning panoramic views.
A beautiful Zen Buddhist temple known for its exquisite gardens, bamboo grove, and seasonal night illuminations, offering a peaceful retreat.
The centerpiece of this park is a massive, famous cherry tree that is illuminated at night and surrounded by festive food stalls during blossom season.
Find a vendor selling matcha-flavored ice cream, a staple treat in this area, perfect for a refreshing break while exploring Higashiyama.
A serene, cherry-tree-lined walking route connecting Nanzen-ji to Ginkaku-ji, allowing visitors to happen upon various smaller shrines.
A renowned traditional teahouse offering exquisite matcha, wagashi, and other Japanese sweets in a serene setting.
Experience a traditional tea ceremony or simply enjoy matcha in one of the serene teahouses within the vast Daitoku-ji Temple complex.
Dubbed the 'king of sentō' (public baths), this is one of Kyoto's oldest bathhouses, featuring classic early-20th-century architecture.
An ancient and important Shinto shrine, notable for its Tatezuna sand cones.
The magnificent, world-famous gilded (golden) pavilion, reflecting beautifully in its surrounding pond.
A peaceful cafe located near Kinkaku-ji, offering a quiet spot to enjoy a drink and light snack after visiting the Golden Pavilion.
A shrine containing a massive grove of plum trees. If you visit in late winter, the blossoms offer a highly fragrant alternative to cherry blossoms.
Enjoy a bowl of freshly made soba noodles at a local eatery, a simple yet delicious Japanese staple, perfect for a casual meal.
A large Rinzai Zen Buddhist temple complex, it is the parent temple to Taizo-in and Shunko-in.
Savor Shojin Ryori, traditional Buddhist vegetarian cuisine, often served at temples or specialized restaurants, offering a unique and healthy dining experience.
Another sub-temple of Myoshin-ji, this temple also provides authentic Zen Buddhist meditation sessions for visitors.
These sub-temples of Myoshin-ji offer authentic Zen Buddhist meditation sessions, teaching the true meaning of the practice to visitors.
A sub-temple of Myoshin-ji, it offers authentic Zen Buddhist meditation sessions, teaching the true meaning of the practice to visitors.
A massive World Heritage temple with large grounds that are absolutely spectacular during the spring blooms, featuring beautiful gardens and pagodas.
A massive World Heritage temple with large grounds that are absolutely spectacular during the spring blooms.
This area has a deep, centuries-old history of local brewing, making it a significant cultural spot for sake production.
Located in the Fushimi area, this historic 400-year-old local brewery produces famous sake and offers facility tours.
Located just south of Kyoto station, this temple is famous for its towering pagoda which stands tall over beautiful gardens.
Explore the vibrant street food stalls leading up to Fushimi Inari-taisha Shrine, offering a variety of local snacks and treats.
An iconic Shinto shrine famous for its thousands of vibrant orange torii gates that wind through the forest up Mount Inari.
A collection of sake breweries and tasting rooms in the Fushimi area, perfect for exploring Kyoto's rich sake brewing heritage.
Enjoy a traditional Japanese unagi (eel) dish at a local restaurant in the Fushimi area, known for its rich flavors and delicate preparation.
This historic, 400-year-old local brewery produces some of Kyoto's most famous sake and offers facility tours and tastings.
A large Zen temple famous for its spectacular autumn foliage and traditional Zen gardens, offering a peaceful and beautiful experience.
Located just south of Kyoto Station, this temple is famous for its towering pagoda which stands tall over beautiful gardens, a UNESCO World Heritage site.
A large portion of the mountainside is bright with cherry blossoms, offering breathtaking natural beauty.
The streets around Arashiyama Station are lined with authentic, non-tacky local shops selling traditional crafts, fans, and sweets.
The area around Hankyu Arashiyama Station is filled with local food stalls, especially vibrant at night during blossom seasons.
Also known as the Moss Temple, this is another stunning World Heritage site located in the western hills.
Visitors can enjoy viewing the cherry blossoms on the mountainside from this scenic train that travels through Arashiyama.
One of Kyoto's stunning World Heritage sites, this temple is nestled into the beautiful western hills of Arashiyama.
An active film studio and the traditional home of Japanese period dramas, where you can walk through authentic outdoor samurai movie sets.
A 700-year-old street food market featuring unique local delicacies like baby octopus and soy donuts.
A quirky neighborhood thoroughfare, also known as Ichijo-dori, haunted by dozens of homemade monsters (yokai).
An off-the-beaten-path Buddhist temple guarded by twelve hundred unique and expressive stone carvings of rakan.
A temple famous for its 1,001 life-sized, carved statues of the Buddhist Goddess of Mercy, Kannon, each with a unique face.
Home to the quintessential Japanese rock garden, a masterpiece of Zen culture that has existed for more than 500 years.
A unique Starbucks housed in a traditional Japanese machiya, taking coffee drinkers back in time with tatami mat seating.
Established over a thousand years ago, this family-run teahouse by Imamiya Shrine serves skewers of sweet, roasted rice cake.
A tunnel-like walkway on the 10th floor of the modern Kyoto Station building, offering panoramic views of the city.
A 450-year-old candy shop with a ghostly legend, selling simple amber-colored sweets said to have nourished a ghost's child.
A 131-meter-tall observation tower across from Kyoto Station, offering 360-degree views of the city and surrounding mountains.
One of Japan's premier art museums, focusing on pre-modern Japanese and Asian art in its striking modern and historic buildings.

Zoom in on an interesting pattern or detail of a modern building.
Capture the neon glow and vibrant lights of the city after dark.
Take a photo that tells a story about daily life and commuting in Central Kyoto.
Find the most unusual or surprising item for sale in a vending machine and snap a picture.
Find a small shrine or quiet spot tucked away amidst the urban hustle and capture its tranquility.
Take a picture of the most interesting or delicious-looking bowl of ramen you can find.

Take a close-up shot of maple or pine leaves against a temple backdrop.

Get a close-up shot of weathered wood, mossy stone, or a traditional tiled roof.
Capture the interesting and dramatic shadows cast by temple structures or trees.
Photograph a traditional sweet, like mochi or dango, from a local teahouse.
Find a single vibrant detail, like a red gate or a colorful charm, against a neutral background.
Take a photo that visually conveys the peaceful, quiet atmosphere of the area.

Take a macro shot of a unique leaf or flower, focusing on its texture and details.

Take a selfie on a street that looks like it's been preserved from another era.
Take a photo looking into a shop or restaurant through its traditional fabric doorway curtain (noren).
Get a close-up shot of a traditional texture, like a clay wall, wooden lattice, or stone pavement.
Capture the details of wooden prayer plaques (ema) or paper fortunes (omikuji) at a shrine.
Find a high point on a sloped street and capture the sea of traditional rooftops below.
Photograph a local craft item, like pottery, a fan, or a parasol, in a shop window.
Capture an image that you feel perfectly encapsulates the traditional charm of the Higashiyama and Gion area.
